revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[en:Fukiishi]][[fr:Fukiishi]][[ja:葺石]][[zh:葺石]] {{for|the model and actress|Kazue Fukiishi}} in Kobe covered with restored ''fukiishi'']] '''{{transl|ja|Fukiishi}}''' ({{lang|ja|葺石}} or {{lang|ja|葺き石}} "roofing stone") were a means of covering burial chambers and [[Kofun|Kofuns]] during the [[kofun period]] of Japan ({{circa|250–538}}). Stones collected from riverbeds were affixed to the slopes of raised [[kofun]] and other burial chambers. They are considered to have descended from forms used in {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi-period|1=Yayoi period}} tumuli. They are common in the early and mid-Kofun periods, but most late Kofun-period tumuli do not have them. ==Origin and ancestry== ]] Tombs covered with fukiishi appear sporadically in Western Japan from the mid-{{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} and continue into the Kofun period.{{sfn|Ishino|1990|p=5}} Fukiishi are thought to be one element of the characteristics of the period of kofun at the time that they were making their first appearance; what are thought of as the oldest examples of what was to lead the generally fixed form are seen at {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|qid=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} and the presumed slightly older [[Hokenoyama Kofun]] in the city of {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|qid=Q127026|lt=Sakurai|1=Sakurai, Nara}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}.{{sfn|Shiraishi|2002|p=49}} Neither fukiishi nor {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} accompany mounds from before regularization such as at the {{illm|Makimuku ruins|lt=Makimuku keyhole-shaped kofun group|ja|纒向型前方後円墳}}. The ''{{transl|ja|hariishi}}'' ({{lang|ja|貼石}}) seen at the {{illm|Yosumi Tosshutsu funkyūbo|ja|四隅突出型墳丘墓|1=X-shaped Burial Mound}} ("four corner projections type grave mound") in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|qid=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}} in Western Japan are often put forth as an ancestor of ''{{transl|ja|fukiishi}}''. The perimeter of the foot of kofun No. 3 of [[Nishidani Kofun cluster]] in {{ill|Izumo|en|Izumo|qid=Q301580|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo}} in {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}} is completely covered with ''{{transl|ja|hariishi}}''. The Kofun at the [[:en:Tatetsuki|Tatetsuki]] archaeological site in the city of {{ill|Sōja|en|Sōja|qid=Q873536|lt=Sōja|1=Sōja}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}} is surrounded with rows of stones; such examples are widely seen in the San'in and the nearby {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|qid=Q398087|lt=San'yō region|1=San'yō region}}s, where examples of Kofuns demarcated by stacks of {{ill|stone wall|en|stone wall|qid=Q979531|lt=stone wall|1=Stone wall}}s are also seen.{{sfn|Kondō|2001|p=141}} ==Terminology== In his report on an excavation in 1915 on mound No. 21 of [[:en:Saitobaru kofungun|Saitobaru kofungun]] Kofun group in what is now the city of {{ill|Saito, Miyazaki|en|Saito, Miyazaki|qid=Q850479|lt=Saito|1=Saito, Miyazaki}} in {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, historian {{illm|Ryū Imanishi|ja|今西龍}} titled an entry ''{{transl|ja|hyōmen no fukiishi}}'' ("surface roofing stones"){{efn|{{lang|ja|表面の葺石}} ''{{transl|ja|hyōmen no fukiishi}}'' "surface roofing stones"}} in which he described the condition of the ''{{transl|ja|fukiishi}}'' in a ground plan and cross-section.{{sfn|Ishizuka|1992|p=60}} The term ''{{transl|ja|fukiishi}}'' came into use as an archaeological term largely due to the influence of {{illm|Kenji Takahashi (archaeologist)|ja|高橋健自|lt=Kenji Takahashi's}} book ''{{transl|ja|Kofun to jōbunka}}'' (''"Kofun and ancient culture"'', 1922).{{efn|{{cite book |last = Takahashi |first = Kenji |title = Kofun to jōdai bunka |trans-title = Kofun and ancient culture |script-title = ja:古墳と上代文化 |year = 1922 |publisher = Tokyo Kokushi Kōshūkai}} }} Via examples of kofun in the {{ill|Kinai|en|Kinai|qid=Q906708|lt=Kinai|1=Kinai}} capital region Takashi described the ''{{transl|ja|fukiishi}}'' there as serving both practical and decorative purposes: in practical terms the use of pebbles provided protection from the wind, rain, and cold, while the stones served to beautify the mound which was after all built above ground to attract public attention and to impress and rouse piety in visiting worshippers.{{sfn|Ishizuka|1992|p=60–61}} ==Research== , {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}} ]] in {{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|qid=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}} is roofed with fukiishi made up of riverbed stones.]] Excavation of fukiishi themselves was nearly nonexistent before World War II. It was only after the war that scientific examination, verification, and surveying of the stones took place.{{sfn|Aoki|2003|p=179}} In 1953, {{illm|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} led a group of specialists, locals, and students in an excavation at the {{illm|Tsukinowa kofun|ja|月の輪古墳}} in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|en|Misaki, Okayama|qid=Q1156100|lt=Misaki|1=Misaki, Okayama}}{{efn|At the time named {{illm|Yūkason|ja|飯岡村 (岡山県)}} }} in Okayama. The group surveyed three quarters of the kofun, with a height of 10 m and a diameter of 60 m. The report published in 1960 gave the extent of distribution, shape and size, {{ill|Petrology|en|Petrology|qid=Q163082|lt=petrological|1=Petrology}} analysis, technical analysis of the roofing method, and minute illustration the configuration of the individual stones. The report estimated the number of stones at about 80,000.{{sfn|Ishizuka|1992|p=61}} In response to the rapid increase in the post-war period of large-scale development that led to in the destruction of historical ruins, urgent excavations were carried out with the goal of thorough examination rather than as the preservation of documentation. As a result of this early research, Shōzō Haraguchi and {{illm|Tadashi Nishitani|ja|西谷正}} published a paper in 1967 on the excavation of tumulus C1 of the Bentenyama kofungun group in [[:en:Takatsuki, Osaka|Takatsuki]] in Osaka. The paper notes the results of investigation into the stacking methods, the weight and number of stones per unit area, the collection areas and transportation routes of the stones, and other details.{{sfn|Haraguchi|Nishitani|1967}} {{illm|Tadashi Aoki|ja|青木敬}} stated that following this report hardly any other resulting from such an accumulation of verifiable details on fukiishi has appeared.{{sfn|Aoki|2003|p=179}} Research into fukiishi has come to require knowledge related to construction technology.{{sfn|Ishizuka|1992|p=62}} To enable the scientific qualification and quantification of research documentation, Hisanori Ishizuka has called for research on:{{sfn|Ishizuka|1992|pp=62–67}} # {{ill|Paleoecology|en|Paleoecology|qid=Q1337995|lt=Palaeoecology|1=Paleoecology}} # {{ill|Soil mechanics|en|Soil mechanics|qid=Q471872|lt=Soil mechanics|1=Soil mechanics}} # {{ill|Structural engineering|en|Structural engineering|qid=Q633538|lt=Structural engineering|1=Structural engineering}} # Materials # Fukiishi construction # Structural masonry ==Materials and construction methods== The material for fukiishi was often made up of pebbles and stones from dry riverbeds. In the case of pebbles, a variety of stones was used. For example, the late-4th-century [[Matsuokayama Kofun]] in [[:en:Kashiwara, Osaka|Kashiwara]] in Osaka Prefecture was made with {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|qid=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} flagstones in vertical or sloped piles.{{sfn|Ishino|1992|p={{page needed|date=November 2015}} }} Other sites used {{ill|Chert|en|Chert|qid=Q1070438|lt=Chert|1=Chert}}, {{ill|Sandstone|en|Sandstone|qid=Q13085|lt=Sandstone|1=Sandstone}}, {{ill|slate|en|slate|qid=Q207079|lt=slate|1=Slate}}, {{ill|Basalt|en|Basalt|qid=Q43338|lt=Basalt|1=Basalt}}, or other types of stone. Embankments were often made with soil transported from other areas. {{ill|Palynology|en|Palynology|qid=Q212919|lt=Pollen analysis|1=Palynology}} of the fifth- and sixth-century kofuns of the {{ill|Kuboizumi-Maruyama Historic Sites|ja|久保泉丸山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuboizumi-Maruyama Historic Sites|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Saga, Saga|Saga]] has shown clear disparities in the vegetation in the soil of the mound faces and that of the surrounding environment. This example suggests that the {{ill|borrow pit|en|borrow pit|qid=Q778964|lt=borrow pit|1=Borrow pit}}s for the construction of the mounds were in remote locations.{{sfn|Ishizuka|1992|p=67}} === Stone Building Methods === Among different methods of stone tomb construction, it was found that there are four different classifications of Kofun structures. ==== Type 1 ==== These buildings include a stacked stone wall with thick backfill and lack a base stone in the construction. The typical examples of burials in this style are Nakayama Otsuka Kofun (Nara Prefecture [[:en:Tenri City|Tenri City]]), Hokeno Mountain Kofun (Sakurai City, Nara Prefecture), and in the rear section of the former Inari Tomaru, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, these are the oldest structures still present in Yamato. ==== Type 2 ==== This construction method is characterized by the dual-staged stacking of the stone base. The basal stone is stacked horizontally and the subsequent masonry is stacked on top. These mounds tend to exhibit steep inclines of 30 degrees or more. Prime examples of this type of Kofun are found in Red Tsuchiyama Kofun in the Tenri City of Nara Prefecture and Xizhiduka Mound and the Miwa Mountain Kofun 1 in {{ill|Tsuyama|en|Tsuyama|qid=Q841125|lt=Tsuyama|1=Tsuyama}} City Okayama Prefecture. ==== Class 3-1 ==== "Stacking Stone" is the classification of this type of stone, the basement stones are overlapped with the main body of the structure. The Benten Mountain Mound (C1) in Takatsuki City, Osaka Prefecture is a representative example of this style of mound building. ==== Class 3-2 ==== This type of Kofun does not stack stone layers over the foundation. Instead, the stones are embedded in the soil in a method referred to as "pasting stones". ==== Class 3-3 ==== Construction technology changes as current methods fuse with local methods. Examples of this can be found in [[:en:Kashiwabara City|Kashiwabara City]] at Mt Tama 1 and Tama 7 (Tamane Mountain Kofuns). ==== Class 4 ==== This method of construction follows the "paste stone" method in Class 3-2. Mounds generally displays shallow inclines of 20-25 degrees. The mounds consist mostly of small stones with gravel filled gaps; which is generally found in the Saki Gouzanyama Kofun (Saki Gouran Mountain Kofun) in Nara City, Nara Prefecture. {{Expand section|date=April 2017}} ==Purpose and function== in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}
Half of the restored mound is covered with fukiishi.]] The purpose of ''{{transl|ja|fukiishi}}'' is seen, as in Kenji Takahashi's account of 1922, to protect the Kofun while projecting its majesty.{{sfn|Ōtsuka|Kobayashi|1982|p=345}} As they were used primarily on slopes and rarely on flat surfaces it is also thought they may have served to prevent runoff, and may have contributed to waterproofing and drainage. They may also have been used to imply the mound was sacred, holy ground and clearly differentiate it from adjacent areas.{{sfn|Kondō|2001|p=141}} Archaeologist {{illm|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} notes the use of ''{{transl|ja|fukiishi}}'' may be related to a desire to make royalty visible and exhibit the people's connection with royalty, a feature he sees as characteristic of Japanese Kofuns in comparison with those of other Asian countries, and in particular in the case of keyhole-shaped kofun.{{sfn|Hirose|2003|pp=134, 179–180}} {{Expand section|date=May 2015}} ==Demise== in {{ill|Fuchū, Tokyo|en|Fuchū, Tokyo|qid=Q208818|lt=Fuchū|1=Fuchū, Tokyo}} in Tokyo]] The scale of kofun construction peaked in the middle of the Kofun period in the mid-5th century, reflecting the power and influence of the political structure and the social status of the entombed via the form and scale of the tumuli. Towards the close of the 5th century, the construction of large keyhole-shaped kofun and groups of layered kofun waned. At the beginning of the 6th century, the scale of most keyhole-shaped kofun west from the Kantō region became smaller, the smaller {{ill|Baichō|ja|陪塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baichō|1=Baizuka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11658220}} ("satellite") tumuli began to disappear, and fukiishi began to be used infrequently. Three-tiered kofun saw a strong decrease in favour of two-tiered ones. Outside of the Kantō region, {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} stopped being used.{{sfn|Hirose|2003|pp=32–33}} From the end of the 6th century to the beginning of the 7th, the tumuli of the monarchs changed from [[Square Kofun|square ''hōfun'' tumuli]] to octagonal [[Octagonal Kofun|''hakkaku-fun'']] tumuli.{{sfn|Niiro|1992|p=160}} Amongst these, [[Joenkahofun|''jōenkahōfun'']] dome-shaped grave mounds made with fukiishi, such as [[Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja Kofun]] in {{ill|Fuchū, Tokyo|en|Fuchū, Tokyo|qid=Q208818|lt=Fuchū|1=Fuchū, Tokyo}} in Tokyo, are noteworthy. Nevertheless, during this era kofun were quickly ceding the role of central ritual building to {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}s, and the adoption of {{ill|Rammed earth|en|Rammed earth|qid=Q1363009|lt=Rammed earth|1=Rammed earth}} construction techniques stands out. With regards to {{ill|kofun of the final period|ja|終末期古墳|1=Late Kofun tumuli|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun of the final period|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Kofun tumuli|qq=}}, ''{{transl|ja|gaigo resseki}}''{{efn|{{lang|ja|外護列石}} }} stones lined up to trace the line of the mound are recognized, and what could be called ''fukiishi'' are almost never seen.{{sfn|Iwasaki|1992|p=8}} ''{{transl|ja|Gaigo resseki}}'' were not piled on the mounds as ''fukiishi'' were, nor were the construction techniques of ''fukiishi'' applied to them. Aoki has pointed out that this was modeled on the decoration techniques of the foundations of Buddhist temples.{{sfn|Aoki|2003|p=188}} ==Kofun restoration== {{Empty section|date=May 2015}} {{clear}} ==Gallery== goshiki_the_evening.jpg|Goshikizuka in the evening Kamezukakohun 01.jpg|Restored [[Kamezuka Kofun]] in [[:en:Ōita, Ōita|Ōita]]|alt= Kamezukakohun p01.jpg|Kamezuka from the south Odome-arako kofun01.jpg|Restored {{ill|Ōdome Arako Kofun|ja|大留荒子古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdome Arako Kofun|qq=}} in {{ill|Kasugai, Aichi|en|Kasugai, Aichi|qid=Q26101|lt=Kasugai, Aichi|1=Kasugai, Aichi}}|alt= Shionjiyama-Kohun001.JPG|[[Shionjiyama Kofun]] in Osaka|alt= Shionjiyama Kohun, Yao02.jpg|Shionjiyama Kofun ==Notes== {{Notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist|colwidth=20em}} ==Works cited== {{Refbegin|colwidth=40em}} * {{cite book |last = Aoki |first = Takashi |title = Kofun chikuzō no kenkyū—funkyū kara mita kofun no chiikisei |trans-title = Research on the construction of kofun |script-title = ja:古墳築造の研究－墳丘からみた古墳の地域性－ |publisher = Rokuichi Shobō |year = 2003 |isbn = 4-947743-16-6 }} * {{cite book |last1 = Haraguchi |first1 = Shōzō |last2 = Nishitani |first2 = Tadashi |title = Bentenyama kofungun no chōsa |trans-title = Research on Bentenyama kofungun |script-title = ja:弁天山古墳群の調査 |chapter = 弁天山C1号墳 |publisher = Ōsaka-fu kyōiku iinkai |year = 1967 |isbn = }} * {{cite book |last = Hirose |first = Kazuo |title = Zenpō-kōen-fun kokka |trans-title = Keyhole-shaped kifun nation |script-title = ja:前方後円墳国家 |publisher = [[Kadokawa Shoten]] |year = 2003 |isbn = 4-04-703355-3 }} * {{cite book |last = Ishino |first = Hironobu |title = Kofun Jidai-shi |trans-title = History of the Kofun Period |script-title = ja:古墳時代史 |publisher = Yuzankaku |year = 1990 |isbn = 4-639-01888-6 }} * {{cite book |editor-last = Ishino |editor-first = Hironobu |title = Kofun jidai no kenkyū 7 Kofun 1 Funkyū to naibu kōzō |trans-title = Kofun period research 7 Kofun 1 Kofuns and their inner structure |script-title = ja:古墳時代の研究7 古墳I 墳丘と内部構造 |volume = 7 |location = 図版 |publisher = Yuzankaku |year = 1992 |isbn = 4-639-00960-7 }} * {{cite book |last = Ishizuka |first = Hisanori |title = Kofun jidai no kenkyū 7 Kofun 1 Funkyū to naibu kōzō |trans-title = Kofun period research 7 Kofun 1 Kofuns and their inner structure |script-title = ja:古墳時代の研究7 古墳I 墳丘と内部構造 |chapter = 3 外部施設 2 葺石 |publisher = Yuzankaku |year = 1992 |isbn = 4-639-00960-7 }} * {{cite book |last = Iwasaki |first = Takuya |author-link = Takuya Iwasaki |chapter = 1 総論 |editor-last = Ishino |editor-first = Hironobu |title = Kofun jidai no kenkyū 7 Kofun 1 Funkyū to naibu kōzō |trans-title = Kofun period research 7 Kofun 1 Kofuns and their inner structure |script-title = ja:古墳時代の研究7 古墳I 墳丘と内部構造 |volume = 7 |location = 図版 |publisher = Yuzankaku |year = 1992 |isbn = 4-639-00960-7 }} * {{cite book |last = Kondō |first = Yoshirō |title = Zenpō-kōen-fun ni manabu |trans-title = Learning from keyhole-shaped kofun |script-title = ja:前方後円墳に学ぶ |publisher = Yamakawa Shuppansha |year = 2001 |isbn = 4-634-60490-6 }} * {{cite book |last = Mori |first = Kōichi |title = Kyodai kofun—Zenpō-kōen-fun no nazo wo toku |trans-title = Giant kofun—solving the mystery of the keyhole-shaped kofun |script-title = ja:巨大古墳—前方後円墳の謎を解く |publisher = Sōshisha |year = 1985 |isbn = 4-7942-0220-2 }} * {{cite book |last = Mori |first = Kōichi |chapter = 「ヤマト古墳文化の成立」 |pages = 225–274 |title = Nihon no kodai 5: Zenpō-kōen-fun no seiki |trans-title = Ancient Japan 5: The era of keyhole-shaped kofun |script-title = ja:日本の古代5 前方後円墳の世紀 |editor-last = Mori |editor-first = Kōichi |publisher = Chūō Kōronsha |year = 1986a |isbn = 4-12-402538-6 }} * {{cite book |last = Mori |first = Kōichi |chapter = 「海と陸のあいだの前方後円墳」 |pages = 275–300 |title = Nihon no kodai 5: Zenpō-kōen-fun no seiki |trans-title = Ancient Japan 5: The era of keyhole-shaped kofun |script-title = ja:日本の古代5 前方後円墳の世紀 |editor-last = Mori |editor-first = Kōichi |publisher = Chūō Kōronsha |year = 1986b |isbn = 4-12-402538-6 }} * {{cite book |last = Niiro |first = Izumi |chapter = V古墳時代 2古墳 a前・中期 |title = Zukai Nihon no jinrui iseki |trans-title = |script-title = ja:図解･日本の人類遺跡 |publisher = Tokyo Diagaku Shuppankai |year = 1992 |isbn = 4-13-026200-9 }} * {{cite book |last1 = Ōtsuka |first1 = Hatushige |last2 = Kobayashi |first2 = Saburō |title = Kofun Jiten |trans-title = Kofun Dictionary |script-title = ja:古墳辞典 |publisher = Tokyo-dō Publishing |year = 1982 |isbn = 4-490-10165-1 }} * {{cite book |last = Shiraishi |first = Taichirō |title = Nihon no Jidai-shi: Wakoku Tanjō |trans-title = Period History of Japan: Birth of Wakoku |volume = 1 |script-title = ja:「倭国誕生」白石編『日本の時代史1 倭国誕生』 |publisher = Yoshikawa Kōbunkan |year = 2002 |isbn = 4-642-00801-2 }} {{Refend}} ==External links== * {{Commons category-inline}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20150513001616/http://www.hi-net.zaq.ne.jp/bucdm105/ Goshikizuka and Ōtoshiyama Kofun website] (in Japanese) * [http://kofunmoodys.fc2web.com/gunma_hotoda.htm Kofun Moody's] (in Japanese) {{Portal bar|History|Japan}} Fukiishi {{Kofun navbar}} [[File:Goshikizuka kofun02s3200.jpg|upright=1.3|thumb|alt=|The round end of [[Goshikizuka (Sentsubo) Kofun|Goshikizuka Kofun]] [[File:Nishidani Graves 02.JPG|right|thumb|alt=|What are thought to be proto-fukiishi at Nishidani kofun No. 3 in {{ill|Izumo|en|Izumo|qid=Q301580|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo}} [[Image:Tsukinowa Kofun 01.JPG|thumb|alt=|{{illm|Tsukinowa kofun|ja|月の輪古墳}} in {{ill|Misaki, Okayama|en|Misaki, Okayama|qid=Q1156100|lt=Misaki|1=Misaki, Okayama}} [[Image:Jirobeeduka1goukofun-1.JPG|thumb|Jirobēzuka mound No. 1 in {{ill|Kani, Gifu|en|Kani, Gifu|qid=Q853805|lt=Kani|1=Kani, Gifu}} [[File:nagare_tomb_kubire.JPG|thumb|The early 5th-century Nagare-san kofun, of the {{illm|Umami kofungun|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster}} group in {{ill|Kawai, Nara|en|Kawai, Nara|qid=Q1203880|lt=Kawai|1=Kawai, Nara}} [[File:Musashi Fuchū Kumano Jinja kofun 1.jpg|thumb|alt=|Restored kofun at [[Kumano shrine]] [[en:Fukiishi]] {{Commons category|Fukiishi}} [[fr:Fukiishi]] [[ja:葺石]] [[zh:葺石]] {{wikidata link|Q11621213}} [[Category:Archaeology of death]] [[Category:Japanese architectural history]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Kofun|*]] [[Category:Mounds]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
